exercises.title: Read this EXPLAIN ANALYZE output — what's wrong?
exercises.instructions:
```
Seq Scan on payments  (cost=0.00..98432.00 rows=12 width=180)
  (actual time=0.017..4198.341 ms)
  Filter: ((merchant_id = 'MRC-001') AND (status = 'PENDING'))
  Rows Removed by Filter: 4987891
Planning Time: 1.2 ms
Execution Time: 4199.0 ms
```
1. What is the problem?
2. What type of scan is this?
3. How many rows were scanned vs returned?
4. What index would fix this?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. PROBLEM: Sequential scan (Seq Scan) — PostgreSQL reads every row in the entire table to find matches.

2. Seq Scan: reads all rows from disk in order. No index used.
   Contrast with Index Scan: directly jumps to matching rows via B-tree index.

3. Scanned: 4,987,891 + 12 = 4,987,903 rows total
   Returned: 12 rows
   Ratio: 415,658:1 waste — reading 415K rows to return each 1!

4. Fix: composite index with equality columns first:
   CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_payments_merchant_status
     ON payments(merchant_id, status);
   
   OR partial index (if only querying PENDING):
   CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_payments_merchant_pending
     ON payments(merchant_id)
     WHERE status = 'PENDING';
   → After: Index Scan, ~0.3ms instead of 4199ms (14,000× faster)

test_cases.expected:
1. PROBLEM: Sequential scan (Seq Scan) — PostgreSQL reads every row in the entire table to find matches.

2. Seq Scan: reads all rows from disk in order. No index used.
   Contrast with Index Scan: directly jumps to matching rows via B-tree index.

3. Scanned: 4,987,891 + 12 = 4,987,903 rows total
   Returned: 12 rows
   Ratio: 415,658:1 waste — reading 415K rows to return each 1!

4. Fix: composite index with equality columns first:
   CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_payments_merchant_status
     ON payments(merchant_id, status);
   
   OR partial index (if only querying PENDING):
   CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_payments_merchant_pending
     ON payments(merchant_id)
     WHERE status = 'PENDING';
   → After: Index Scan, ~0.3ms instead of 4199ms (14,000× faster)

hints: Rows Removed by Filter: how many rows scanned but didn't match
Seq Scan = no index on the filtered columns
Equality columns first, range last in composite index

exercises.title: Complete the optimized index creation
exercises.instructions: Fill in: composite index with CONCURRENTLY for production safety.

blanks.hint: ORDER BY created_at DESC — index direction matches sort

hints: CONCURRENTLY: no lock, but slower to build
DESC on index: matches ORDER BY DESC queries — avoids sort step

exercises.title: Write a Flyway migration adding a composite index
exercises.instructions:
Write V8__add_payment_search_index.sql:
1. Index on payments(merchant_id, created_at DESC)
2. Partial index for PENDING status only
3. CONCURRENTLY for both
4. Comment explaining each index purpose
exercises.expected_solution:
-- V8__add_payment_search_index.sql

-- Index 1: Composite index for merchant + date queries
-- Powers: WHERE merchant_id = ? AND created_at > ?
-- Used by: payment listing, reconciliation, dashboard
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_payments_merchant_date
  ON payments(merchant_id, created_at DESC);

-- Index 2: Partial index for PENDING status only
-- Only ~5% of payments are PENDING → 95% smaller than full status index
-- Powers: WHERE merchant_id = ? AND status = 'PENDING'
-- Used by: pending payment alerts, retry logic
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_payments_merchant_pending
  ON payments(merchant_id, created_at DESC)
  WHERE status = 'PENDING';

-- Note: CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a transaction block.
-- Flyway: set spring.flyway.mixed=true to allow DDL+DML in same migration
-- OR: run CONCURRENTLY index creation in separate migration from transactional DDL
hints: IF NOT EXISTS: idempotent migration (safe to re-run)
CONCURRENTLY + Flyway: cannot be in a transaction — use mixed=true or separate migration
Partial index WHERE clause: same as query WHERE clause for best performance

exercises.title: Spring Data JPA @QueryHints for read-only queries
exercises.instructions:
Write a Spring Data repository with @QueryHints:
1. findByMerchantIdAndStatusAndCreatedAtAfter with HINT_READONLY
2. @Query with JOIN FETCH to avoid N+1
3. setHint(HINT_FETCH_SIZE) for large result sets
4. Explain: what each hint does
hints: HINT_READONLY: tells Hibernate entity is read-only — no change tracking
HINT_FETCH_SIZE: controls JDBC cursor batch size, not result set limit
JOIN FETCH: single query with JOIN instead of N+1 lazy loads

exercises.title: Find slow queries with pg_stat_statements
exercises.instructions:
Write the SQL queries to:
1. Find top 10 slowest queries by average execution time
2. Find queries with the most rows examined vs returned
3. Reset stats after fixing a query
4. Show: how to enable pg_stat_statements in PostgreSQL
exercises.expected_solution:
-- SETUP: Enable pg_stat_statements (in postgresql.conf):
-- shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'
-- pg_stat_statements.track = all
-- Then: CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_stat_statements;

-- 1. Top 10 slowest queries by AVERAGE execution time:
SELECT
  query,
  calls,
  round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS avg_ms,
  round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms,
  round(rows / calls::numeric, 1) AS avg_rows
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- 2. Queries with most I/O (high shared_blks_read = lots of disk reads):
SELECT
  query,
  calls,
  round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS avg_ms,
  shared_blks_read / NULLIF(calls, 0) AS avg_blocks_read
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY shared_blks_read DESC
LIMIT 10;
-- High avg_blocks_read with low avg_rows = missing index!

-- 3. Reset stats (after fixing a query, start fresh measurement):
SELECT pg_stat_statements_reset();

-- 4. In Spring Boot application.yml — enable slow query logging as backup:
-- spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.session.events.log.LOG_QUERIES_SLOWER_THAN_MS: 100
-- Logs any Hibernate query taking > 100ms
-- (complement to pg_stat_statements — works without DB config access)
hints: pg_stat_statements: most valuable PostgreSQL performance extension
mean_exec_time: average query time — best metric for slow query detection
shared_blks_read: pages read from disk — high = missing index or cold cache

exercises.title: Keyset pagination vs OFFSET pagination
exercises.instructions:
Razorpay payment list: page 500 of 20 results (OFFSET 10,000) is slow.
1. Show the OFFSET problem
2. Implement keyset pagination (WHERE id > last_id)
3. Spring Data JPA keyset repository method
4. Trade-offs: when OFFSET is acceptable
hints: OFFSET N: database scans and discards N rows before returning
Keyset WHERE id > lastId: index seek directly to position
Keyset limitation: no random page access — must navigate sequentially

exercises.title: @SpringBootTest: verify query uses index (no N+1)
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest that:
1. Uses @DataJpaTest with H2
2. Inserts 5 merchants and 50 payments
3. Uses Hibernate StatisticsService to count queries
4. Verifies: findWithMerchantSince fires 1 query (not N+1)
5. Asserts: result size and no extra queries
hints: Statistics.getQueryExecutionCount(): Hibernate query counter
stats.clear() in @BeforeEach: reset count between tests
JOIN FETCH: produces 1 SQL with JOIN instead of 1 + N selects

exercises.title: Design indexes for Razorpay payment reconciliation
exercises.instructions:
Razorpay reconciliation queries:
1. Daily totals by gateway: GROUP BY gateway, status, DATE(created_at)
2. Failed payments by merchant: WHERE status='FAILED' AND merchant_id=? AND created_at BETWEEN ? AND ?
3. Recent PENDING by amount > 10000: WHERE status='PENDING' AND amount > 10000

For each: design the optimal index (composite, partial, covering). Add to Flyway.
exercises.expected_solution:
-- V9__reconciliation_indexes.sql

-- Query 1: Daily totals by gateway + status
-- GROUP BY gateway, status, DATE(created_at)
-- Best: index covering the GROUP BY columns (no table access for aggregation)
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_payments_gateway_status_date
  ON payments(gateway, status, created_at)
  INCLUDE (amount);  -- covering index: amount included → Index Only Scan for SUM(amount)!

-- Query 2: Failed payments by merchant + date range
-- WHERE status='FAILED' AND merchant_id=? AND created_at BETWEEN ? AND ?
-- Partial index: only FAILED (small subset), equality columns first
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_payments_merchant_failed_date
  ON payments(merchant_id, created_at DESC)
  WHERE status = 'FAILED';  -- partial: only index FAILED rows

-- Query 3: Recent PENDING by amount threshold
-- WHERE status='PENDING' AND amount > 10000
-- Partial index (only PENDING) + composite:
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_payments_pending_large
  ON payments(amount DESC, created_at DESC)
  WHERE status = 'PENDING' AND amount > 10000;  -- only index the qualifying rows!
-- Very small index: only PENDING + large amount rows
hints: INCLUDE: covering index — columns included for retrieval but not for B-tree structure
Partial WHERE = query WHERE: enables Index Only Scan for that specific query pattern
Equality first, range last: merchant_id, created_at (not created_at, merchant_id)

exercises.title: Batch insert optimization with JPA
exercises.instructions:
Insert 10,000 payment records efficiently:
1. Spring Data JPA saveAll with batch size
2. application.yml: hibernate.jdbc.batch_size=50
3. Compare: 10,000 individual saves vs batched
4. @SpringBootTest: verify batch saves complete in < 2 seconds
hints: hibernate.jdbc.batch_size: 50 inserts per JDBC batch (round trip)
hibernate.order_inserts: true: group same-table inserts together for better batching
saveAll() vs save() in a loop: saveAll batches; individual save() doesn't

exercises.title: Which query is faster and why?
exercises.instructions:
Table: payments (50M rows). Index on (merchant_id, created_at DESC).

Query A:
```sql
SELECT id, amount, status FROM payments
WHERE merchant_id = 'MRC-001'
ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;
```

Query B:
```sql
SELECT * FROM payments
WHERE merchant_id = 'MRC-001'
ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;
```

Query C:
```sql
SELECT id, amount, status FROM payments
WHERE merchant_id = 'MRC-001' OR status = 'PENDING'
ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;
```

Rank fastest to slowest and explain.
exercises.expected_solution:
FASTEST: Query A
  Uses the composite index (merchant_id, created_at DESC).
  SELECT id, amount, status: if all 3 columns are in the index or INCLUDE,
  PostgreSQL can do an 'Index Only Scan' — never touches the table heap.
  Result: sub-millisecond for 20 rows.

SECOND: Query B
  Uses the same composite index (merchant_id, created_at DESC).
  SELECT *: fetches all columns → requires table heap access after index lookup.
  'Index Scan' instead of 'Index Only Scan' — slightly slower than A.
  Still fast: < 5ms for 20 rows (index narrows to matching rows, then fetches).

SLOWEST: Query C
  WHERE merchant_id = 'MRC-001' OR status = 'PENDING'
  OR condition: PostgreSQL cannot use a single index for both conditions.
  Plans: Bitmap Index Scan (two separate index scans merged) or Seq Scan.
  Without index on status: Seq Scan of all 50M rows.
  With index on (merchant_id) and index on (status): Bitmap Index Scan
  → scans both indexes, merges bitmaps → still reads many table rows.
  Result: likely 100ms+ depending on PENDING row count.
  
Fix for C: rewrite as UNION ALL:
  SELECT id, amount, status FROM payments WHERE merchant_id = 'MRC-001'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT id, amount, status FROM payments WHERE status = 'PENDING'
    AND merchant_id != 'MRC-001'  -- avoid duplicates

test_cases.expected:
FASTEST: Query A
  Uses the composite index (merchant_id, created_at DESC).
  SELECT id, amount, status: if all 3 columns are in the index or INCLUDE,
  PostgreSQL can do an 'Index Only Scan' — never touches the table heap.
  Result: sub-millisecond for 20 rows.

SECOND: Query B
  Uses the same composite index (merchant_id, created_at DESC).
  SELECT *: fetches all columns → requires table heap access after index lookup.
  'Index Scan' instead of 'Index Only Scan' — slightly slower than A.
  Still fast: < 5ms for 20 rows (index narrows to matching rows, then fetches).

SLOWEST: Query C
  WHERE merchant_id = 'MRC-001' OR status = 'PENDING'
  OR condition: PostgreSQL cannot use a single index for both conditions.
  Plans: Bitmap Index Scan (two separate index scans merged) or Seq Scan.
  Without index on status: Seq Scan of all 50M rows.
  With index on (merchant_id) and index on (status): Bitmap Index Scan
  → scans both indexes, merges bitmaps → still reads many table rows.
  Result: likely 100ms+ depending on PENDING row count.
  
Fix for C: rewrite as UNION ALL:
  SELECT id, amount, status FROM payments WHERE merchant_id = 'MRC-001'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT id, amount, status FROM payments WHERE status = 'PENDING'
    AND merchant_id != 'MRC-001'  -- avoid duplicates

hints: Index Only Scan: all columns in index → no table access
OR: usually prevents index use — rewrite as UNION ALL
SELECT * vs SELECT cols: only matters if covering index is available

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay query optimization
exercises.instructions:
Build complete optimization for Razorpay payment search:
1. Repository with @QueryHints (readonly, fetch size)
2. JOIN FETCH to avoid N+1
3. Keyset pagination method
4. @DataJpaTest (2 tests): no N+1, keyset returns correct results
hints: JOIN FETCH in JPQL: Hibernate produces 1 SQL with JOIN instead of N+1
Statistics.getQueryExecutionCount(): confirms 1 query after JOIN FETCH
Keyset: WHERE p.id < :cursor ORDER BY id DESC → consistently O(log n) via PK index

exercises.title: Monitor slow queries in Spring Boot
exercises.instructions:
Wire slow query logging into Micrometer:
1. Configure hibernate.session.events.log queries slower than 100ms
2. Register a custom Timer for query timing via Hibernate Interceptor
3. @SpringBootTest: verify slow query monitoring is active
4. Prometheus alert rule for slow query rate
hints: LOG_QUERIES_SLOWER_THAN_MS: Hibernate's built-in slow query log
Custom Timer: wraps query execution for metric recording
Slow query counter: drives alert rule (rate > 0.1/s = pattern of slowness)

exercises.title: Zerodha order search — full optimization stack
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Zerodha order search optimization:
1. Flyway migration: composite index on (symbol, order_type, created_at DESC)
2. Repository with JOIN FETCH + @QueryHints
3. Keyset pagination
4. @DataJpaTest (3 tests): no N+1, keyset works, query timer records
5. All 3 tests pass
hints: @DataJpaTest: auto-configures in-memory H2 and JPA layer only
Statistics clear in @BeforeEach: fresh query count per test
MeterRegistry available in @DataJpaTest with spring.jpa properties

exercises.title: Read replica routing in Spring Data
exercises.instructions:
Route read queries to a read replica:
1. Configure two DataSources (primary + replica) in application.yml
2. @Transactional(readOnly=true) → routes to replica
3. AbstractRoutingDataSource for dynamic routing
4. Show: how this scales read throughput
5. @SpringBootTest: verify routing works
hints: AbstractRoutingDataSource: Spring's hook for dynamic DataSource selection
isCurrentTransactionReadOnly(): true when @Transactional(readOnly=true) active
Read replica: 80% of queries are reads — replicas handle them, freeing primary

exercises.title: Complete M22 T2 synthesis — query optimization pipeline
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Razorpay query optimization pipeline:
1. Flyway migration with 2 optimized indexes
2. Repository with JOIN FETCH + @QueryHints + keyset pagination
3. QueryMetricsService: Timer for query duration
4. @DataJpaTest (3 tests): single query (no N+1), keyset works, metrics recorded

All 3 tests pass.
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M22 T2 query optimization synthesis
hints: @Import(SynthQueryMetrics.class): makes the service available in @DataJpaTest
Statistics.getQueryExecutionCount(): 1 for keyset (direct JPA query)
timer.count(): increases by 1 after each metrics.time() call
